summary: Gruber challenges the longstanding consensus that iPad Pros are great hardware hamstrung by limited software, proposing an inverted perspective: if iPadOS is accepted as a deliberately simple, appliance-style platform beloved by millions, then the iPad Pro's purpose is to make that experience as premium as possible rather than to enable desktop-class workflows. He argues that 'pro' in iPad Pro means 'deluxe' rather than 'professional,' with Apple optimizing for thinness, lightness, and display quality over raw computational need. The M4 chip's extraordinary performance serves power efficiency and the tandem OLED display rather than demanding workloads. He draws analogies to Lexus vs. Toyota and first-class vs. coach, arguing premium hardware for a simple platform is no more absurd than premium experiences in any other domain.
key_points: The consensus that iPad Pros have great hardware but limited software may be wrong-headed because it starts from a hardware-first perspective rather than accepting iPadOS on its own terms.
iPad Pro 'pro' means deluxe — thinner, lighter, nicer displays — while MacBook Pro 'pro' means faster and more capable, reflecting fundamentally different platform philosophies.
The M4 appears in iPad Pro before MacBook Air likely due to TSMC production constraints and because only the M4 can drive the tandem OLED display at the iPad Pro's extreme thinness.
With the new Magic Keyboard, a 13-inch iPad Pro now weighs within 20g of a MacBook Air, eliminating a longstanding weight penalty.
Many users genuinely love iPadOS's guardrails as liberating constraints, and dismissing their preferences because power users feel limited is myopic.
money_quotes: Incredibly powerful and beautiful hardware hamstrung by infuriatingly limited software.
Twice as fast for the same energy consumption is the Mac way of thinking. Same performance with half the energy consumption is the iOS way of thinking.
A better Mac is faster. Nicer too, but primarily faster. A better iPad is nicer. Faster, too, but primarily nicer.
There is tremendous power in having a computer that is simple not merely by suggestion but by hard and fast technical constraints.

Those who espouse this opinion are often so adamant that such an arrangement would be both an undeniably good idea for users and 'easy' for Apple to do that they are convinced that the only possible explanation for why Apple hasn't done this is that it's all part of a scheme to sell both an iPad and MacBook.
Arguing that 'no one can do real work on an iPad' reminds me of Yogi Berra's 'No one goes there anymore, it's too crowded.'
It's the heaviness of the Mac that allows iOS to remain light.
And optimizing for niceness is underrated.
key_insight: The iPad Pro's purpose is not to be a powerful workstation constrained by weak software, but to be the most premium possible expression of a deliberately simple computing platform — and optimizing for niceness over raw capability is a legitimate product philosophy.
